speaker: interviewer
text: --teaches in the rec center.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah? I bet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- yeah. And we [INAUDIBLE] our house is [INAUDIBLE]. Every year-- you don't see it so much. But every year we-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Nice. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. I'm going to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, we're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Susan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] --she goes in the kitchen to eat. So what are we going to do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she's got to eat. Yeah. Well, if she's as quiet as possible. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to put someone outside the door, not to ring the bell?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, have one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe the woman can help, you know, make sure that she doesn't walk in by accident or something. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we can close these--

speaker: interviewer
text: We can close those. OK, slide.

speaker: interviewer
text: January 17, 1997. The survivor is Susan Brunn. Her maiden name was Mendel. I'm interviewing her. My name is Anne Bernard. In Flushing, New York, United States of America. The interview is taking place in English. Today is January 17, 1997. I'm interviewing Susan Brunn. Her maiden name was Mendel. My name is Anne Bernard. The interview is taking place in Flushing, New York, in the United States of America. And the interview is taking place in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan Brunn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell it? Your last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-U-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan Mendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Mendel?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-E-N-D-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 22, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 73 years old, for another month.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ernst.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what your father did for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an actor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in the special-- in the special company in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He wasn't. And I-- I don't know. He was not in Berlin, I think. He comes from a town called Szczecin. But I really don't know where he worked, or what-- how long he worked. He was very young when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 28 or 29.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he die from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an officer in the German army, in the First World War, and sustained some wounds, which never healed properly. And he died on the effect of those wounds, in 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your mother after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what. What do you mean by what happened? She lived. And she was, of course, devastated, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] had to go on with her life, and had to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a living did she have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure about the first few years. I was too-- too little. I don't know. But she used to work at all kind of things. She was mainly a sales lady in a dry goods stores. And then she opened her own store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a store did she open?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also dry goods. Ladies' underwear and linens. It was all combined in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] Elegant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the street that it was on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I-- it was-- I think it was Muller Strasse. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How successful was she in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she made a good living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took care of you when she was in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- for the first-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. When I was maybe one or two, I went to live with my aunt and uncle in Raciborz, in Silesia. And when I was nine or 10-- I-- I don't know exactly how old I was. When my mother got me back to Berlin, she put me in a in a home in-- about an hour out of Berlin, where she could come and visit me Sundays. And I was there until I was about 13. And then she took me home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about some of your recollections about living with your aunt and uncle in Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was wonderful, whatever I remember. They were wealthy. They had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a beautiful home. They had a big cigar factory, which came from my grandfather, actually. And I had a very good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Felix and Maria Froehlich.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they related to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Felix was my mother's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other children were in that family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had no children. That's why they-- they would have liked to keep me always, but it didn't work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of your memories of life in-- in their home? Could you give us some details about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I very recall-- very, very little recollection about that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just know that a very embarrassing moment was when I had my first day of school. I was driven to school with a chauffeur. And I felt at that time very embarrassed. And I know that they had a car, one or two cars, at all times. And I was driven around. And I had a very, very comfortable, nice life. I had a nanny who took care of me. I remember certain things. But I remember some stupid things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I remember that they went on a trip for a day or two to Czechoslovakia, which was very near. And I was used to go with them, and I wanted to go with them. But they put me to a cousin-- I think to a cousin next door, or a couple of doors away. And we had to play together and stay together. And I went on the balcony, and I wanted to throw myself down because they didn't take me along. This I see in front of me. It's funny that something stick with you all your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of religion did they practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. They were Jews. My aunt was not Jewish. My aunt was Catholic. But she was a better Jew than many Jews themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about what the first few years in school were like for you in Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember anything about that. I just know I went to school there. Regular--

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate certain holidays with the Froehlichs?

speaker: interviewee
text: They celebrated every holiday, a Jewish one and a non-Jewish one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us some of those experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have any recollection. I just know that there was another brother living in Raciborz, which was a younger brother of my uncle Felix. And they were extremely Jewish. And they were Zionists. And all the Jewish holidays were celebrated in their house. And I remember when we were there, each of us had a menorah, and had on Hanukkah to light the candles. And there were two children, my cousin Maya, and my cousin Erwin. They live now in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate Christmas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my aunt did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But they never had a tree or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us in-- in-- when you went back to live with your mother, what that was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to the home, or when I went with-- to live with her?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both. Well, the home I didn't like. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- well, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I disliked living there. I-- I don't-- I didn't like it. And when I came home to my mother, I was happier. And-- but I don't really remember much how it was. I just know that we always stuck together then. And we lived together from then on, always, all the time. Even in my-- part of my married life, in my first marriage. And it was nothing extraordinary. It was just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] living. I don't know. I don't remember very much, probably because it wasn't so very interesting or whatever. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of an orphanage was it, that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish-- from some kind of a Jewish organization. And it had either children there who had only one parent, or no parents at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What didn't you like about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything. [LAUGHS] The first few months were bearable, because there were very nice people who were in charge. And I remember even their name. We called them Tante-- Tante Ida and Uncle Arnie. They ran the place. And after a short while, they either immigrated or left. I don't know. And there came a woman who ran the place, a very Polish, elderly lady. I see her in front of me, but I don't know her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you were in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been about eight or nine years old. I'm not sure. Nine?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. In to a regular public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language that you spoke in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn any other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started learning in school, English and French.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to see your mother and-- and stayed with her, could you tell us what kind of relationship you had with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very good one. I was so glad to be home again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a school did you go to then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also to public school. Never into a private school, if-- if that's what you mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Berlin like in those years, as-- as you can recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] From what I hear from all people, and what I could see, as little as I could see, it was very thriving, and very international city. It had lots of culture. And it was nice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it turned sour when Hitler really started [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't nice anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the neighborhood that you lived in with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Wait a minute. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the neighborhood of the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [GERMAN], I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For-- we-- we moved around quite a bit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know for what reason. But we did. I remember 1, 2, 3 different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ordinary apartments. And at one point, the last one, my mother had to-- the rents were very expensive. And she didn't have the income like that anymore. So she rented out two or three rooms. Besides, it was also a fact that Jews were not allowed to have an apartment for themselves. They had to share.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first recollection of antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I think it was in school, from one of my teachers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember. But I think that was it. It was a known-- known fact. I mean, you-- you-- it was-- it was forced antisemitism. It was told. People have been told the Jews are this and the Jews are that. And you were not so much aware of being different in the beginning. You were much more assimilated. And suddenly, you were a different person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only that one teacher. All the others were pretty nice. And especially our principal was a very nice man. And I was at a point, I think, the last Jewish girl in school. And he always try to keep me. And he said-- I remember once he said to me, Mendel, I want you to finish. I want you to be able to have an education. And I will try not to report-- they had to report. And I will try not to report as long as I can possibly do it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all what I remember. And I remember his name. It was Dr. Michaelis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you the last Jewish person, or child, in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think that-- we did not immigrate. That a lot of my friends and our acquaintances did immigrate. And I would love to go out. And I told my mother, I would like to get out of Germany too. And she always said, nothing will happen to us. Your father was an officer in the German army, and I'm a-- considered a war widow. And nothing will happen to us. And when it was really happening, it was too late. She had no connection to get out. She tried everything. I know she paid money for a visa to-- to Cuba. And this didn't work. And then she tried to go to Chile. And nothing. It was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the information that you were getting in Berlin about what was happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were all frightened. We saw that it's not getting better, it's getting worse. And we were forced to-- to work in forced labor fields, in-- in factories, and in-- in all kind of institutions, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with very little or-- very, very little pay. And then little by little, it got every-- every day worse. You had to wear the Star. And you had to-- we're not allowed to sit on this bench or do this. We're not allowed to use subways or trams. If you lived in a certain mileage from your job, you got a pass that you could use the subway or the bus at certain hours. Otherwise, you had to walk. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's actually all I remember. Everybody knows it got worse and worse. And then we were replaced, even in the factories, by Russian and Polish prisoners of war. And they didn't need us anymore, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue with that, I'd like to go back and ask you about what you did to entertain yourself as a young girl in Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: To entertain myself? We had friends. And we met-- you mean after the-- when the Nazis started in '33?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a youngster, when you were living with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- that was already after 1933. So we had friends. And we stuck together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we had a fairly normal life until 1937, '38. '37, yeah. '38. We went swimming, and we went to the beach. And we went and visited each other. And we had little home parties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the beach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berlin is a city with lots of water. There was Wannsee, the Little Wannsee, the Big Wannsee. Oh, a lot of beaches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in school, in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to the theater in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. I remember once that I went to a theater to saw a-- and I saw At-- I think at the [GERMAN], or some kind of [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read any Jewish newspapers or German newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had like a daily German newspaper. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't even recall what kind of papers we had or any-- I was too young for that. I-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother talk about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very limited. Very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get a feeling that-- that things were getting worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: By seeing it, by experiencing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that we were then forbidden to go into a movie. We were forbidden to go to the beach. We were to-- forbidden to do almost anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were singled out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that you were forbidden to go to the beach, and to go to the movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were announcements. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were in the papers, and over the radio. And there-- there must have been a Jewish paper where all the laws were printed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone you know try to defy the laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I was aware of. I mean, they-- I-- in the beginning, it was possible because you didn't have to wear the Star of David. So you took a chance to use a subway, or to do this or-- you know, you-- you didn't get caught immediately all the time. But once you-- I don't even remember when that was. I think it was 1940 or '41, when you had to start wearing the yellow star. And even then, some people didn't sew it on the coat. They put it on with a certain way, that you could take it off and put it on, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and tried to, you know, go a little bit against the law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did that too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you wear it, and when did you not?

speaker: interviewee
text: 90% of the time. Only when I wanted to sneak into a movie or meet my friends at a certain place where we were not allowed. We did it. Or we wore our handbag like this, that you didn't see it. I-- I really don't remember everything about that. Because this is somehow that you-- that you don't want to keep that. You don't want to think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Judaism practiced in your home at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you mean if we light-- lit candles on Friday? No, we did not. My mother did not do that, because she felt she's alone and there's no husband. I don't know why. But it wasn't done. Except that in the home where I was, we did it all the time. And in the house by my aunt and uncle, we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother practice the-- following the Sabbath and the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: She knew it was a holiday, and she went to occasionally to a temple on the high holy days, but that was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us some things about some of your friends? And what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I would say that 80% of my friends got killed in the Holocaust. And the other 20% immigrated [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to Israel, to South America, and to here. I have friends still here, in the United States, in New York. And I have one girl I'm very friendly with. I still correspond with her. She's a Gentile girl, in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first meet Klaus?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942. Or was it '41, at the end of '41? I don't remember. It could be in the beginning of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you met him, had you stopped school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What grade did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to figure it out. I don't know when we went to school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stopped school in '38. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] So I was 15 years old. In the-- the first or second year of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] high-- it was different in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was considered already lyceum. They-- they don't have that here. What is that called here? High-- not high school. It's already higher than high school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what it's called. What were you-- what subjects were you learning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything in general, and-- whatever you're being taught in school, plus languages. English and French was our-- the language we had to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: The funny thing is when I went to Hebrew school, I liked Biblical history. And I liked history altogether, and Greek mythology I liked. I was never crazy about arithmetic. And French was awful. English I picked up well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you stop school at 15?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had to. I was thrown out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was I 15? '38, '23, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I had to-- what did I do, actually? Nothing. Oh, yes. I tried to learn a trade. I went to school and learned manicuring, and-- because we always thought we might immigrate. And that didn't work out. I was-- wanted to become a cosmetician. And I was-- had an interview with the offices of Elizabeth Arden. And they were not allowed to employ me, because I was Jewish. And then I went through somebody, or through a friend of ours. I don't remember. To somebody who taught me to become a seamstress, to-- to learn sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll pick up on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
